    53→const (
    54→	// longThrottleLatency defines threshold for logging requests. All requests being
    55→	// throttled (via the provided rateLimiter) for more than longThrottleLatency will
    56→	// be logged.
    57→	longThrottleLatency = 50 * time.Millisecond
    58→
    59→	// extraLongThrottleLatency defines the threshold for logging requests at log level 2.
    60→	extraLongThrottleLatency = 1 * time.Second
    61→)
    62→
    63→// HTTPClient is an interface for testing a request object.
    64→type HTTPClient interface {
    65→	Do(req *http.Request) (*http.Response, error)
    66→}
    67→
    68→// ResponseWrapper is an interface for getting a response.
    69→// The response may be either accessed as a raw data (the whole output is put into memory) or as a stream.
    70→type ResponseWrapper interface {
    71→	DoRaw(context.Context) ([]byte, error)
    72→	Stream(context.Context) (io.ReadCloser, error)
    73→}
    74→
    75→// RequestConstructionError is returned when there's an error assembling a request.
    76→type RequestConstructionError struct {
    77→	Err error
    78→}
    79→
    80→// Error returns a textual description of 'r'.
    81→func (r *RequestConstructionError) Error() string {
    82→	return fmt.Sprintf("request construction error: '%v'", r.Err)
    83→}
    84→
    85→var noBackoff = &NoBackoff{}
    86→
    87→type requestRetryFunc func(maxRetries int) WithRetry
    88→
    89→func defaultRequestRetryFn(maxRetries int) WithRetry {
    90→	return &withRetry{maxRetries: maxRetries}
    91→}
    92→
    93→// Request allows for building up a request to a server in a chained fashion.
    94→// Any errors are stored until the end of your call, so you only have to
    95→// check once.
    96→type Request struct {
    97→	c *RESTClient
    98→
    99→	contentConfig     ClientContentConfig
   100→	contentTypeNotSet bool
   101→
   102→	warningHandler WarningHandlerWithContext
   103→
   104→	rateLimiter flowcontrol.RateLimiter
   105→	backoff     BackoffManagerWithContext
   106→	timeout     time.Duration
   107→	maxRetries  int
   108→
   109→	// generic components accessible via method setters
   110→	verb       string
   111→	pathPrefix string
   112→	subpath    string
   113→	params     url.Values
   114→	headers    http.Header
   115→
   116→	// structural elements of the request that are part of the Kubernetes API conventions
   117→	namespace    string
   118→	namespaceSet bool
   119→	resource     string
   120→	resourceName string
   121→	subresource  string
   122→
   123→	// output
   124→	err error
   125→
   126→	// only one of body / bodyBytes may be set. requests using body are not retryable.
   127→	body      io.Reader
   128→	bodyBytes []byte
   129→
   130→	retryFn requestRetryFunc
   131→}
   132→
   133→// NewRequest creates a new request helper object for accessing runtime.Objects on a server.
   134→func NewRequest(c *RESTClient) *Request {
   135→	var backoff BackoffManagerWithContext
   136→	if c.createBackoffMgr != nil {
   137→		backoff = c.createBackoffMgr()
   138→	}
   139→	if backoff == nil {
   140→		backoff = noBackoff
   141→	}
   142→
   143→	var pathPrefix string
   144→	if c.base != nil {
   145→		pathPrefix = path.Join("/", c.base.Path, c.versionedAPIPath)
   146→	} else {
   147→		pathPrefix = path.Join("/", c.versionedAPIPath)
   148→	}
   149→
   150→	var timeout time.Duration

    57→// ReflectorStore is the subset of cache.Store that the reflector uses
    58→type ReflectorStore interface {
    59→	// Add adds the given object to the accumulator associated with the given object's key
    60→	Add(obj interface{}) error
    61→
    62→	// Update updates the given object in the accumulator associated with the given object's key
    63→	Update(obj interface{}) error
    64→
    65→	// Delete deletes the given object from the accumulator associated with the given object's key
    66→	Delete(obj interface{}) error
    67→
    68→	// Replace will delete the contents of the store, using instead the
    69→	// given list. Store takes ownership of the list, you should not reference
    70→	// it after calling this function.
    71→	Replace([]interface{}, string) error
    72→
    73→	// Resync is meaningless in the terms appearing here but has
    74→	// meaning in some implementations that have non-trivial
    75→	// additional behavior (e.g., DeltaFIFO).
    76→	Resync() error
    77→}
    78→
    79→// TransformingStore is an optional interface that can be implemented by the provided store.
    80→// If implemented on the provided store reflector will use the same transformer in its internal stores.
    81→type TransformingStore interface {
    82→	ReflectorStore
    83→	Transformer() TransformFunc
    84→}

    86→// Reflector watches a specified resource and causes all changes to be reflected in the given store.
    87→type Reflector struct {
    88→	// name identifies this reflector. By default, it will be a file:line if possible.
    89→	name string
    90→	// The name of the type we expect to place in the store. The name
    91→	// will be the stringification of expectedGVK if provided, and the
    92→	// stringification of expectedType otherwise. It is for display
    93→	// only, and should not be used for parsing or comparison.
    94→	typeDescription string
    95→	// An example object of the type we expect to place in the store.
    96→	// Only the type needs to be right, except that when that is
    97→	// `unstructured.Unstructured` the object's `"apiVersion"` and
    98→	// `"kind"` must also be right.
    99→	expectedType reflect.Type
   100→	// The GVK of the object we expect to place in the store if unstructured.
   101→	expectedGVK *schema.GroupVersionKind
   102→	// The destination to sync up with the watch source
   103→	store ReflectorStore
   104→	// listerWatcher is used to perform lists and watches.
   105→	listerWatcher ListerWatcherWithContext
   106→	// backoff manages backoff of ListWatch
   107→	backoffManager wait.BackoffManager
   108→	resyncPeriod   time.Duration
   109→	// minWatchTimeout defines the minimum timeout for watch requests.
   110→	minWatchTimeout time.Duration
   111→	// clock allows tests to manipulate time
   112→	clock clock.Clock
   113→	// paginatedResult defines whether pagination should be forced for list calls.
   114→	// It is set based on the result of the initial list call.
   115→	paginatedResult bool
   116→	// lastSyncResourceVersion is the resource version token last
   117→	// observed when doing a sync with the underlying store
   118→	// it is thread safe, but not synchronized with the underlying store
   119→	lastSyncResourceVersion string
   120→	// isLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable is true if the previous list or watch request with
   121→	// lastSyncResourceVersion failed with an "expired" or "too large resource version" error.
   122→	isLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable bool
   123→	// lastSyncResourceVersionMutex guards read/write access to lastSyncResourceVersion
   124→	lastSyncResourceVersionMutex sync.RWMutex
   125→	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error.
   126→	watchErrorHandler WatchErrorHandlerWithContext
   127→	// WatchListPageSize is the requested chunk size of initial and resync watch lists.
   128→	// If unset, for consistent reads (RV="") or reads that opt-into arbitrarily old data
   129→	// (RV="0") it will default to pager.PageSize, for the rest (RV != "" && RV != "0")
   130→	// it will turn off pagination to allow serving them from watch cache.
   131→	// NOTE: It should be used carefully as paginated lists are always served directly from
   132→	// etcd, which is significantly less efficient and may lead to serious performance and
   133→	// scalability problems.
   134→	WatchListPageSize int64
   135→	// ShouldResync is invoked periodically and whenever it returns `true` the Store's Resync operation is invoked
   136→	ShouldResync func() bool

   138→	MaxInternalErrorRetryDuration time.Duration
   139→	// useWatchList if turned on instructs the reflector to open a stream to bring data from the API server.
   140→	// Streaming has the primary advantage of using fewer server's resources to fetch data.
   141→	//
   142→	// The old behaviour establishes a LIST request which gets data in chunks.
   143→	// Paginated list is less efficient and depending on the actual size of objects
   144→	// might result in an increased memory consumption of the APIServer.
   145→	//
   146→	// See https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-api-machinery/3157-watch-list#design-details
   147→	useWatchList bool
   148→}

it is random in [minWatchTimeout, 2*minWatchTimeout].\nvar defaultMinWatchTimeout = 5 * time.Minute\n\n// ReflectorStore is the subset of cache.Store that the reflector uses\ntype ReflectorStore interface {\n\t// Add adds the given object to the accumulator associated with the given object's key\n\tAdd(obj interface{}) error\n\n\t// Update updates the given object in the accumulator associated with the given object's key\n\tUpdate(obj interface{}) error\n\n\t// Delete deletes the given object from the accumulator associated with the given object's key\n\tDelete(obj interface{}) error\n\n\t// Replace will delete the contents of the store, using instead the\n\t// given list.     33→// This file implements a low-level controller that is used in
    34→// sharedIndexInformer, which is an implementation of
    35→// SharedIndexInformer.  Such informers, in turn, are key components
    36→// in the high level controllers that form the backbone of the
    37→// Kubernetes control plane.  Look at those for examples, or the
    38→// example in
    39→// https://github.com/kubernetes/client-go/tree/master/examples/workqueue
    40→// .
    41→
    42→// Config contains all the settings for one of these low-level controllers.
    43→type Config struct {
    44→	// The queue for your objects - has to be a DeltaFIFO due to
    45→	// assumptions in the implementation. Your Process() function
    46→	// should accept the output of this Queue's Pop() method.
    47→	Queue
    48→
    49→	// Something that can list and watch your objects.
    50→	ListerWatcher
    51→
    52→	// Process can process a popped Deltas.
    53→	Process ProcessFunc
    54→
    55→	// ProcessBatch can process a batch of popped Deltas, which should return `TransactionError` if not all items
    56→	// in the batch were successfully processed.
    57→	//
    58→	// For batch processing to be used:
    59→	// * ProcessBatch must be non-nil
    60→	// * Queue must implement QueueWithBatch
    61→	// * The client InOrderInformersBatchProcess feature gate must be enabled
    62→	//
    63→	// If any of those are false, Process is used and no batch processing is done.
    64→	ProcessBatch ProcessBatchFunc
    65→
    66→	// ObjectType is an example object of the type this controller is
    67→	// expected to handle.
    68→	ObjectType runtime.Object
    69→
    70→	// ObjectDescription is the description to use when logging type-specific information about this controller.
    71→	ObjectDescription string
    72→
    73→	// FullResyncPeriod is the period at which ShouldResync is considered.
    74→	FullResyncPeriod time.Duration
    75→
    76→	// MinWatchTimeout, if set, will define the minimum timeout for watch requests send
    77→	// to kube-apiserver. However, values lower than 5m will not be honored to avoid
    78→	// negative performance impact on controlplane.
    79→	// Optional - if unset a default value of 5m will be used.
    80→	MinWatchTimeout time.Duration
    81→
    82→	// ShouldResync is periodically used by the reflector to determine
    83→	// whether to Resync the Queue. If ShouldResync is `nil` or
    84→	// returns true, it means the reflector should proceed with the
    85→	// resync.
    86→	ShouldResync ShouldResyncFunc
    87→
    88→	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error.
    89→	//
    90→	// Contextual logging: WatchErrorHandlerWithContext should be used instead of WatchErrorHandler in code which supports contextual logging.
    91→	WatchErrorHandler WatchErrorHandler
    92→
    93→	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error
    94→	// and WatchErrorHandler is not set.
    95→	WatchErrorHandlerWithContext WatchErrorHandlerWithContext
    96→
    97→	// WatchListPageSize is the requested chunk size of initial and relist watch lists.
    98→	WatchListPageSize int64
    99→}
   100→
   101→// ShouldResyncFunc is a type of function that indicates if a reflector should perform a
   102→// resync or not. It can be used by a shared informer to support multiple event handlers with custom
   103→// resync periods.
   104→type ShouldResyncFunc func() bool
   105→
   106→// ProcessFunc processes a single object.
   107→type ProcessFunc func(obj interface{}, isInInitialList bool) error
   108→
   109→// ProcessBatchFunc processes multiple objects in batch.
   110→// The deltas must not contain multiple entries for the same object.
   111→type ProcessBatchFunc func(deltas []Delta, isInInitialList bool) error
   112→
   113→// `*controller` implements Controller
   114→type controller struct {
   115→	config         Config
   116→	reflector      *Reflector
   117→	reflectorMutex sync.RWMutex
   118→	clock          clock.Clock
   119→}
   120→
   121→// Controller is a low-level controller that is parameterized by a
   122→// Config and used in sharedIndexInformer.
   123→type Controller interface {
   124→	// RunWithContext does two things.  One is to construct and run a Reflector
   125→	// to pump objects/notifications from the Config's ListerWatcher
   126→	// to the Config's Queue and possibly invoke the occasional Resync
   127→	// on that Queue.  The other is to repeatedly Pop from the Queue
   128→	// and process with the Config's ProcessFunc.  Both of these
   129→	// continue until the context is canceled.
   130→	//
   131→	// It's an error to call RunWithContext more than once.
   132→	// RunWithContext blocks; call via go.
   133→	RunWithContext(ctx context.Context)
   134→
   135→	// Run does the same as RunWithContext with a stop channel instead of
   136→	// a context.
   137→	//
   138→	// Contextual logging: RunWithcontext should be used instead of Run in code which supports contextual logging.
   139→	Run(stopCh <-chan struct{})
   140→
   141→	// HasSynced delegates to the Config's Queue
   142→	HasSynced() bool
   143→
   144→	// LastSyncResourceVersion delegates to the Reflector when there
   145→	// is one, otherwise returns the empty string
   146→	LastSyncResourceVersion() string
   147→}

However, values lower than 5m will not be honored to avoid\n\t// negative performance impact on controlplane.\n\t// Optional - if unset a default value of 5m will be used.\n\tMinWatchTimeout time.Duration\n\n\t// ShouldResync is periodically used by the reflector to determine\n\t// whether to Resync the Queue. If ShouldResync is `nil` or\n\t// returns true, it means the reflector should proceed with the\n\t// resync.\n\tShouldResync ShouldResyncFunc\n\n\t// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error.\n\t//\n\t// Contextual logging: WatchErrorHandlerWithContext should be used instead of WatchErrorHandler in code which supports contextual logging.\n\tWatchErrorHandler WatchErrorHandler\n\n\t// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error\n\t// and WatchErrorHandler is not set.\n\tWatchErrorHandlerWithContext WatchErrorHandlerWithContext\n\n\t// WatchListPageSize is the requested chunk size of initial and relist watch lists.\n\tWatchListPageSize int64\n}\n\n// ShouldResyncFunc is a type of function that indicates if a reflector should perform a\n// resync or not.     16→
    17→package cache
    18→
    19→import (
    20→	"errors"
    21→	"fmt"
    22→	"sync"
    23→	"time"
    24→
    25→	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/sets"

    30→
    31→// DeltaFIFOOptions is the configuration parameters for DeltaFIFO. All are
    32→// optional.
    33→type DeltaFIFOOptions struct {
    34→
    35→	// KeyFunction is used to figure out what key an object should have. (It's
    36→	// exposed in the returned DeltaFIFO's KeyOf() method, with additional
    37→	// handling around deleted objects and queue state).
    38→	// Optional, the default is MetaNamespaceKeyFunc.
    39→	KeyFunction KeyFunc
    40→
    41→	// KnownObjects is expected to return a list of keys that the consumer of
    42→	// this queue "knows about". It is used to decide which items are missing
    43→	// when Replace() is called; 'Deleted' deltas are produced for the missing items.
    44→	// KnownObjects may be nil if you can tolerate missing deletions on Replace().
    45→	KnownObjects KeyListerGetter
    46→
    47→	// EmitDeltaTypeReplaced indicates that the queue consumer
    48→	// understands the Replaced DeltaType. Before the `Replaced` event type was
    49→	// added, calls to Replace() were handled the same as Sync(). For
    50→	// backwards-compatibility purposes, this is false by default.
    51→	// When true, `Replaced` events will be sent for items passed to a Replace() call.
    52→	// When false, `Sync` events will be sent instead.
    53→	EmitDeltaTypeReplaced bool
    54→
    55→	// If set, will be called for objects before enqueueing them. Please
    56→	// see the comment on TransformFunc for details.
    57→	Transformer TransformFunc
    58→
    59→	// If set, log output will go to this logger instead of klog.Background().
    60→	Logger *klog.Logger
    61→}
    62→
    63→// DeltaFIFO is like FIFO, but differs in two ways.  One is that the
    64→// accumulator associated with a given object's key is not that object
    65→// but rather a Deltas, which is a slice of Delta values for that
    66→// object.  Applying an object to a Deltas means to append a Delta
    67→// except when the potentially appended Delta is a Deleted and the
    68→// Deltas already ends with a Deleted.  In that case the Deltas does
    69→// not grow, although the terminal Deleted will be replaced by the new
    70→// Deleted if the older Deleted's object is a
    71→// DeletedFinalStateUnknown.
    72→//
    73→// The other difference is that DeltaFIFO has two additional ways that
    74→// an object can be applied to an accumulator: Replaced and Sync.
    75→// If EmitDeltaTypeReplaced is not set to true, Sync will be used in
    76→// replace events for backwards compatibility.  Sync is used for periodic
    77→// resync events.
    78→//
    79→// DeltaFIFO is a producer-consumer queue, where a Reflector is
    80→// intended to be the producer, and the consumer is whatever calls
    81→// the Pop() method.
    82→//
    83→// DeltaFIFO solves this use case:
    84→//   - You want to process every object change (delta) at most once.
    85→//   - When you process an object, you want to see everything
    86→//     that's happened to it since you last processed it.
    87→//   - You want to process the deletion of some of the objects.
    88→//   - You might want to periodically reprocess objects.
    89→//
    90→// DeltaFIFO's Pop(), Get(), and GetByKey() methods return
    91→// interface{} to satisfy the Store/Queue interfaces, but they
    92→// will always return an object of type Deltas. List() returns
    93→// the newest object from each accumulator in the FIFO.
    94→//
    95→// A DeltaFIFO's knownObjects KeyListerGetter provides the abilities
    96→// to list Store keys and to get objects by Store key.  The objects in
    97→// question are called "known objects" and this set of objects
    98→// modifies the behavior of the Delete, Replace, and Resync methods
    99→// (each in a different way).
   100→//
   101→// A note on threading: If you call Pop() in parallel from multiple
   102→// threads, you could end up with multiple threads processing slightly
   103→// different versions of the same object.
   104→type DeltaFIFO struct {
   105→	// lock/cond protects access to 'items' and 'queue'.
   106→	lock sync.RWMutex
   107→	cond sync.Cond
   108→
   109→	// `items` maps a key to a Deltas.
   110→	// Each such Deltas has at least one Delta.
   111→	items map[string]Deltas
   112→
   113→	// `queue` maintains FIFO order of keys for consumption in Pop().
   114→	// There are no duplicates in `queue`.
   115→	// A key is in `queue` if and only if it is in `items`.
   116→	queue []string
   117→
   118→	// populated is true if the first batch of items inserted by Replace() has been populated
   119→	// or Delete/Add/Update/AddIfNotPresent was called first.
   120→	populated bool
   121→	// initialPopulationCount is the number of items inserted by the first call of Replace()
   122→	initialPopulationCount int
   123→
   124→	// keyFunc is used to make the key used for queued item
   125→	// insertion and retrieval, and should be deterministic.
   126→	keyFunc KeyFunc
   127→
   128→	// knownObjects list keys that are "known" --- affecting Delete(),
   129→	// Replace(), and Resync()
   130→	knownObjects KeyListerGetter
   131→
   132→	// Used to indicate a queue is closed so a control loop can exit when a queue is empty.
   133→	// Currently, not used to gate any of CRUD operations.
   134→	closed bool
   135→
   136→	// emitDeltaTypeReplaced is whether to emit the Replaced or Sync
   137→	// DeltaType when Replace() is called (to preserve backwards compat).
   138→	emitDeltaTypeReplaced bool
   139→
   140→	// Called with every object if non-nil.
   141→	transformer TransformFunc
   142→
   143→	// logger is a per-instance logger. This gets chosen when constructing
   144→	// the instance, with klog.Background() as default.
   145→	logger klog.Logger
   146→}
   147→
   148→// TransformFunc allows for transforming an object before it will be processed.
   149→//

All are\n// optional.\ntype DeltaFIFOOptions struct {\n\n\t// KeyFunction is used to figure out what key an object should have. (It's\n\t// exposed in the returned DeltaFIFO's KeyOf() method, with additional\n\t// handling around deleted objects and queue state).\n\t// Optional, the default is MetaNamespaceKeyFunc.\n\tKeyFunction KeyFunc\n\n\t// KnownObjects is expected to return a list of keys that the consumer of\n\t// this queue \"knows about\". It is used to decide which items are missing\n\t// when Replace() is called; 'Deleted' deltas are produced for the missing items.\n\t// KnownObjects may be nil if you can tolerate missing deletions on Replace().\n\tKnownObjects KeyListerGetter\n\n\t// EmitDeltaTypeReplaced indicates that the queue consumer\n\t// understands the Replaced DeltaType. Before the `Replaced` event type was\n\t// added, calls to Replace() were handled the same as Sync(). For\n\t// backwards-compatibility purposes, this is false by default.\n\t// When true, `Replaced` events will be sent for items passed to a Replace() call.\n\t// When false, `Sync` events will be sent instead.\n\tEmitDeltaTypeReplaced bool\n\n\t// If set, will be called for objects before enqueueing them. Please\n\t// see the comment on TransformFunc for details.\n\tTransformer TransformFunc\n\n\t// If set, log output will go to this logger instead of klog.Background().\n\tLogger *klog.Logger\n}\n\n// DeltaFIFO is like FIFO, but differs in two ways.  One is that the\n// accumulator associated with a given object's key is not that object\n// but rather a Deltas, which is a slice of Delta values for that\n// object.  Applying an object to a Deltas means to append a Delta\n// except when the potentially appended Delta is a Deleted and the\n// Deltas already ends with a Deleted.  In that case the Deltas does\n// not grow, although the terminal Deleted will be replaced by the new\n// Deleted if the older Deleted's object is a\n// DeletedFinalStateUnknown.\n//\n// The other difference is that DeltaFIFO has two additional ways that\n// an object can be applied to an accumulator: Replaced and Sync.\n// If EmitDeltaTypeReplaced is not set to true, Sync will be used in\n// replace events for backwards compatibility.  Sync is used for periodic\n// resync events.\n//\n// DeltaFIFO is a producer-consumer queue, where a Reflector is\n// intended to be the producer, and the consumer is whatever calls\n// the Pop() method.\n//\n// DeltaFIFO solves this use case:\n//   - You want to process every object change (delta) at most once.\n//   - When you process an object, you want to see everything\n//     that's happened to it since you last processed it.\n//   - You want to process the deletion of some of the objects.\n//   - You might want to periodically reprocess objects.\n//\n// DeltaFIFO's Pop(), Get(), and GetByKey() methods return\n// interface{} to satisfy the Store/Queue interfaces, but they\n// will always return an object of type Deltas. List() returns\n// the newest object from each accumulator in the FIFO.\n//\n// A DeltaFIFO's knownObjects KeyListerGetter provides the abilities\n// to list Store keys and to get objects by Store key.  The objects in\n// question are called \"known objects\" and this set of objects\n// modifies the behavior of the Delete, Replace, and Resync methods\n// (each in a different way).\n//\n// A note on threading: If you call Pop() in parallel from multiple\n// threads, you could end up with multiple threads processing slightly\n// different versions of the same object.\ntype DeltaFIFO struct {\n\t// lock/cond protects access to 'items' and 'queue'.\n\tlock sync.RWMutex\n\tcond sync.Cond\n\n\t// `items` maps a key to a Deltas.\n\t// Each such Deltas has at least one Delta.\n\titems map[string]Deltas\n\n\t// `queue` maintains FIFO order of keys for consumption in Pop().\n\t// There are no duplicates in `queue`.\n\t// A key is in `queue` if and only if it is in `items`.\n\tqueue []string\n\n\t// populated is true if the first batch of items inserted by Replace() has been populated\n\t// or Delete/Add/Update/AddIfNotPresent was called first.\n\tpopulated bool\n\t// initialPopulationCount is the number of items inserted by the first call of Replace()\n\tinitialPopulationCount int\n\n\t// keyFunc is used to make the key used for queued item\n\t// insertion and retrieval, and should be deterministic.\n\tkeyFunc KeyFunc\n\n\t// knownObjects list keys that are \"known\" --- affecting Delete(),\n\t// Replace(), and Resync()\n\tknownObjects KeyListerGetter\n\n\t// Used to indicate a queue is closed so a control loop can exit when a queue is empty.\n\t// Currently, not used to gate any of CRUD operations.\n\tclosed bool\n\n\t// emitDeltaTypeReplaced is whether to emit the Replaced or Sync\n\t// DeltaType when Replace() is called (to preserve backwards compat).\n\temitDeltaTypeReplaced bool\n\n\t// Called with every object if non-nil.\n\ttransformer TransformFunc\n\n\t// logger is a per-instance logger. This gets chosen when constructing\n\t// the instance, with klog.Background() as default.\n\tlogger klog.Logger\n}\n\n// TransformFunc allows for transforming an object before it will be processed.\n//\n// The most common usage pattern is to clean-up some parts of the object to", "numLines": 150, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 746}}

{"type": "text", "text": "Excellent! Now I have comprehensive information. Let me create a final summary report:

## WORKQUEUE PACKAGE ANALYSIS

### Main Interfaces

**1. TypedInterface[T comparable]** 
   - File: `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/util/workqueue/queue.go`
   - Core workqueue interface with methods:
     - `Add(item T)` - marks item for processing
     - `Get() (item T, shutdown bool)` - blocks until item available
     - `Done(item T)` - marks item processing complete
     - `Len() int` - returns queue length
     - `ShutDown()` - gracefully shuts down queue
     - `ShutDownWithDrain()` - shuts down and waits for all items to process
     - `ShuttingDown() bool` - checks if queue is shutting down

**2. TypedDelayingInterface[T comparable]**
   - File: `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/util/workqueue/delaying_queue.go`
   - Extends TypedInterface with delayed queueing:
     - `AddAfter(item T, duration time.Duration)` - adds item after delay
   - Uses container/heap for priority queue management

**3. TypedRateLimitingInterface[T comparable]**
   - File: `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/util/workqueue/rate_limiting_queue.go`
   - Extends TypedDelayingInterface with rate limiting:
     - `AddRateLimited(item T)` - adds after rate limiter allows
     - `Forget(item T)` - stops tracking item for rate limiting
     - `NumRequeues(item T) int` - returns requeue count

### Key Components

**1. Typed[T comparable] (Basic Queue)**
   - File: `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/util/workqueue/queue.go` (lines 190-371)
   - Implementation using:
     - `queue Queue[T]` - underlying FIFO storage
     - `dirty sets.Set[T]` - items needing processing
     - `processing sets.Set[T]` - currently processing items
     - `sync.Cond` - condition variable for synchronization
   - Features:
     - FIFO ordering
     - Prevents duplicate concurrent processing
     - Requeue items while being processed

**2. delayingType[T comparable]** 
   - File: `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/util/workqueue/delaying_queue.go`
   - Wraps TypedInterface with delay functionality using binary heap

**3. rateLimitingType[T comparable]**
   - File: `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/util/workqueue/rate_limiting_queue.go` (lines 130-147)
   - Wraps TypedDelayingInterface with rate limiter
   - `AddRateLimited()` calls `AddAfter()` with rate limiter's delay

### Rate Limiting Strategies

**File: `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/util/workqueue/default_rate_limiters.go`**

Rate limiter interface:
```
TypedRateLimiter[T comparable] interface {
  When(item T) time.Duration      // returns wait duration
  Forget(item T)                  // clears tracking
  NumRequeues(item T) int         // returns retry count
}
```

**Implementations:**

1. **TypedBucketRateLimiter[T]** (lines 62-77)
   - Token bucket limiter (golang.org/x/time/rate)
   - Global rate limiting (10 qps, 100 bucket size)
   - No per-item tracking

2. **TypedItemExponentialFailureRateLimiter[T]** (lines 84-149)
   - Per-item exponential backoff: `baseDelay * 2^failures`
   - Default: 5ms base delay, 1000s max delay
   - Maps item to failure count
   - Mutex-protected failure tracking

3. **TypedItemFastSlowRateLimiter[T]** (lines 156-206)
   - Fast retries for N attempts, then slow retries
   - Parameters: fast delay, slow delay, max fast attempts
   - Per-item failure counting

4. **TypedMaxOfRateLimiter[T]** (lines 218-259)
   - Composes multiple limiters
   - Returns maximum delay from all limiters
   - **Default controller limiter** (lines 50-56):
     - Combines ItemExponential (5ms-1000s) + Bucket (10 qps)

5. **TypedWithMaxWaitRateLimiter[T]** (lines 266-295)
   - Caps maximum wait time for any limiter
   - Prevents indefinite delays

### How Rate Limiting Works

1. Item is added via `AddRateLimited(item)`
2. `rateLimitingType.AddRateLimited()` calls `rateLimiter.When(item)` to get delay
3. Calls underlying `DelayingInterface.AddAfter(item, delay)`
4. Item is queued in priority heap to be available after delay
5. On successful processing, call `Forget(item)` to clear rate limit tracking
6. If requeued, retry count increments, exponential backoff increases

### Key Files Summary

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/util/workqueue/queue.go` | Core FIFO queue with dirty/processing sets |
| `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/util/workqueue/delaying_queue.go` | Time-based delay wrapper using heap |
| `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/util/workqueue/rate_limiting_queue.go` | Rate limiter wrapper |
| `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/util/workqueue/default_rate_limiters.go` | Limiter implementations (bucket, exponential, fast-slow, max-of) |
| `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/util/workqueue/metrics.go` | Prometheus metrics collection |

---

## LEADERELECTION PACKAGE ANALYSIS

### Main Components

**File: `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/leaderelection.go`**

**1. LeaderElectionConfig** (lines 116-166)
   - Core configuration:
     - `Lock rl.Interface` - resource lock (Lease-based)
     - `LeaseDuration` - how long before non-leader can acquire (default 15s)
     - `RenewDeadline` - leader's renewal timeout (default 10s)
     - `RetryPeriod` - retry interval (default 2s)
     - `Callbacks LeaderCallbacks` - lifecycle callbacks
     - `ReleaseOnCancel bool` - release lease on context cancel
     - `Coordinated bool` - enable coordinated leader election (ALPHA)

**2. LeaderCallbacks** (lines 173-185)
   - `OnStartedLeading(ctx context.Context)` - called when leader acquired
   - `OnStoppedLeading()` - called when leadership lost
   - `OnNewLeader(identity string)` - called when leader changes

**3. LeaderElector** (lines 188-206)
   - Main client with internal state:
     - `observedRecord` - last seen election record
     - `reportedLeader` - previously reported leader
     - `observedTime` - timestamp of observation
     - `clock` - time wrapper for testing
     - `observedRecordLock` - RWMutex for thread safety

### How Leader Election Works

**Run() Flow** (lines 211-222):
1. Call `acquire(ctx)` - loop until leader lease acquired
2. Once leader: spawn goroutine for `OnStartedLeading` callback
3. Call `renew(ctx)` - continuously refresh lease until failure

**Acquire Phase** (lines 252-276):
- Loop calling `tryAcquireOrRenew()` with retry backoff
- Jitter factor: 1.2 (20% random jitter)
- Repeat every RetryPeriod until success

**Renew Phase** (lines 279-307):
- Poll with timeout: `RenewDeadline` / `RetryPeriod`
- Continuously refresh lease record
- If renewal fails, exit and call `OnStoppedLeading`
- If `ReleaseOnCancel=true`, explicitly clear holder identity

**Release** (lines 310-342):
- Get current lease, verify we're the holder
- Update with empty holder identity and minimal duration (1 second)

### Resource Lock Interface

**File: `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/resourcelock/interface.go`**

**1. Interface** (lines 81-100)
   ```go
   type Interface interface {
     Get(ctx context.Context) (*LeaderElectionRecord, []byte, error)
     Create(ctx context.Context, ler LeaderElectionRecord) error
     Update(ctx context.Context, ler LeaderElectionRecord) error
     RecordEvent(string)
     Identity() string
     Describe() string
   }
   ```

**2. LeaderElectionRecord** (lines 46-59)
   - `HolderIdentity` - current lease holder ID
   - `LeaseDurationSeconds` - seconds lease is held
   - `AcquireTime` - when leader acquired lease
   - `RenewTime` - when lease was last renewed
   - `LeaderTransitions` - count of leadership changes
   - `Strategy` - coordinated lease strategy (ALPHA)
   - `PreferredHolder` - preferred leader (ALPHA)

**3. LeaseLock Implementation**
   - File: `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/resourcelock/leaselock.go`
   - Uses Kubernetes `coordination.k8s.io/v1` Lease resource
   - Stores election record in `Lease.Spec` fields
   - Converts between LeaderElectionRecord and LeaseSpec

### Lease-Based Mechanism Details

**LeaseLock Methods:**
- `Get()` (lines 42-54): Fetch Lease, convert spec to LeaderElectionRecord
- `Create()` (lines 57-70): Create new Lease with election record
- `Update()` (lines 73-96): Update Lease spec with new record
- `RecordEvent()` (lines 99-109): Emit Kubernetes events
- `Identity()` (lines 118-120): Return lock holder identity
- `Describe()` (lines 113-115): Return namespace/name for logging

**Conversion Functions:**
- `LeaseSpecToLeaderElectionRecord()` (lines 122-147): Parse Lease.Spec
- `LeaderElectionRecordToLeaseSpec()` (lines 149+): Encode record to Lease.Spec

### Key Behaviors

1. **Clock Skew Tolerance**: Configured by ratio of LeaseDuration to RenewDeadline
   - Example: 60s lease / 30s renewal = tolerate 2x time skew

2. **No Fencing**: Does not guarantee exclusive leader access
   - Relies on timestamps, not authoritative arbitration

3. **Automatic Cleanup**: `release()` zeros out holder identity when stepping down

4. **Thread Safety**: RWMutex protects observed record state

5. **Metrics**: Global factory provides leader-on/leader-off metrics

### Key Files Summary

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/leaderelection.go` | Main LeaderElector, election logic |
| `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/resourcelock/interface.go` | Lock interface, record definition |
| `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/resourcelock/leaselock.go` | Kubernetes Lease-based lock implementation |
| `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/metrics.go` | Prometheus metrics for leader state |
| `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/healthzadaptor.go` | Health check integration |
| `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/leasecandidate.go` | Coordinated election (ALPHA) |"}

     - `reportedLeader` - previously reported leader
     - `observedTime` - timestamp of observation
     - `clock` - time wrapper for testing
     - `observedRecordLock` - RWMutex for thread safety

### How Leader Election Works

**Run() Flow** (lines 211-222):
1. Call `acquire(ctx)` - loop until leader lease acquired
2. Once leader: spawn goroutine for `OnStartedLeading` callback
3. Call `renew(ctx)` - continuously refresh lease until failure

**Acquire Phase** (lines 252-276):
- Loop calling `tryAcquireOrRenew()` with retry backoff
- Jitter factor: 1.2 (20% random jitter)
- Repeat every RetryPeriod until success

**Renew Phase** (lines 279-307):
- Poll with timeout: `RenewDeadline` / `RetryPeriod`
- Continuously refresh lease record
- If renewal fails, exit and call `OnStoppedLeading`
- If `ReleaseOnCancel=true`, explicitly clear holder identity

**Release** (lines 310-342):
- Get current lease, verify we're the holder
- Update with empty holder identity and minimal duration (1 second)

### Resource Lock Interface

**File: `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/resourcelock/interface.go`**

**1. Interface** (lines 81-100)
   ```go
   type Interface interface {
     Get(ctx context.Context) (*LeaderElectionRecord, []byte, error)
     Create(ctx context.Context, ler LeaderElectionRecord) error
     Update(ctx context.Context, ler LeaderElectionRecord) error
     RecordEvent(string)
     Identity() string
     Describe() string
   }
   ```

**2. LeaderElectionRecord** (lines 46-59)
   - `HolderIdentity` - current lease holder ID
   - `LeaseDurationSeconds` - seconds lease is held
   - `AcquireTime` - when leader acquired lease
   - `RenewTime` - when lease was last renewed
   - `LeaderTransitions` - count of leadership changes
   - `Strategy` - coordinated lease strategy (ALPHA)
   - `PreferredHolder` - preferred leader (ALPHA)

**3. LeaseLock Implementation**
   - File: `/workspace/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/resourcelock/leaselock.go`
   - Uses Kubernetes `coordination.k8s.io/v1` Lease resource
   - Stores election record in `Lease.Spec` fields
   - Converts between LeaderElectionRecord and LeaseSpec

### Lease-Based Mechanism Details

**LeaseLock Methods:**
- `Get()` (lines 42-54): Fetch Lease, convert spec to LeaderElectionRecord
- `Create()` (lines 57-70): Create new Lease with election record
- `Update()` (lines 73-96): Update Lease spec with new record
- `RecordEvent()` (lines 99-109): Emit Kubernetes events
- `Identity()` (lines 118-120): Return lock holder identity
- `Describe()` (lines 113-115): Return namespace/name for logging

**Conversion Functions:**
- `LeaseSpecToLeaderElectionRecord()` (lines 122-147): Parse Lease.Spec
- `LeaderElectionRecordToLeaseSpec()` (lines 149+): Encode record to Lease.Spec

### Key Behaviors

1. **Clock Skew Tolerance**: Configured by ratio of LeaseDuration to RenewDeadline
   - Example: 60s lease / 30s renewal = tolerate 2x time skew

2. **No Fencing**: Does not guarantee exclusive leader access
   - Relies on timestamps, not authoritative arbitration

3. **Automatic Cleanup**: `release()` zeros out holder identity when stepping down

4. **Thread Safety**: RWMutex protects observed record state

5. **Metrics**: Global factory provides leader-on/leader-off metrics

amount of time per request when no timeout has been set on a RESTClient.\n\t// Defaults to 32s in order to have a distinguishable length of time, relative to other timeouts that exist.\n\tdefaultTimeout = 32 * time.Second\n\n\t// defaultBurst is the default burst to be used with the discovery client's token bucket rate limiter\n\tdefaultBurst = 300\n\n\tAcceptV1 = runtime.ContentTypeJSON\n\t// Aggregated discovery content-type (v2beta1). NOTE: content-type parameters\n\t// MUST be ordered (g, v, as) for server in \"Accept\" header (BUT we are resilient\n\t// to ordering when comparing returned values in \"Content-Type\" header).\n\tAcceptV2Beta1  = runtime.ContentTypeJSON + \";\" + \"g=apidiscovery.k8s.io;v=v2beta1;as=APIGroupDiscoveryList\"\n\tAcceptV2       = runtime.ContentTypeJSON + \";\" + \"g=apidiscovery.k8s.io;v=v2;as=APIGroupDiscoveryList\"\n\tAcceptV2NoPeer = runtime.ContentTypeJSON + \";\" + \"g=apidiscovery.k8s.io;v=v2;as=APIGroupDiscoveryList;profile=nopeer\"\n\t// Prioritize aggregated discovery by placing first in the order of discovery accept types.\n\tacceptDiscoveryFormats = AcceptV2 + \",\" + AcceptV2Beta1 + \",\" + AcceptV1\n)\n\n// Aggregated discovery content-type GVK.\nvar v2Beta1GVK = schema.GroupVersionKind{Group: \"apidiscovery.k8s.io\", Version: \"v2beta1\", Kind: \"APIGroupDiscoveryList\"}\nvar v2GVK = schema.GroupVersionKind{Group: \"apidiscovery.k8s.io\", Version: \"v2\", Kind: \"APIGroupDiscoveryList\"}\n\n// DiscoveryInterface holds the methods that discover server-supported API groups,\n// versions and resources.\ntype DiscoveryInterface interface {\n\tRESTClient() restclient.Interface\n\tServerGroupsInterface\n\tServerResourcesInterface\n\tServerVersionInterface\n\tOpenAPISchemaInterface\n\tOpenAPIV3SchemaInterface\n\t// Returns copy of current discovery client that will only\n\t// receive the legacy discovery format, or pointer to current\n\t// discovery client if it does not support legacy-only discovery.\n\tWithLegacy() DiscoveryInterface\n}\n\n// AggregatedDiscoveryInterface extends DiscoveryInterface to include a method to possibly\n// return discovery resources along with the discovery groups, which is what the newer\n// aggregated discovery format does (APIGroupDiscoveryList).\ntype AggregatedDiscoveryInterface interface {\n\tDiscoveryInterface\n\n\tGroupsAndMaybeResources() (*metav1.APIGroupList,
